architecture.summary: The paper evaluates off-the-shelf LLM systems (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and prior OpenAI model releases) on tax law reasoning tasks. The input is a tax law question; the system applies few-shot prompting (example question-answer pairs) and optionally retrieves relevant legal authority (tax code sections or regulations) to inject as additional context via text prompt augmentation. The output is the model's text reasoning and answer. No training or fine-tuning of the LLM is performed; all enhancement is via prompt engineering and retrieval-augmented context.

biblio.title: Large language models as tax attorneys: a case study in legal capabilities emergence
biblio.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A Mathematical Physical and Engineering Sciences

claims.statement: LLMs demonstrate emerging legal understanding capabilities, with improved performance in each subsequent OpenAI model release
Retrieving and using relevant legal authority has an impact on performance when provided as additional legal context to LLMs
Few-shot prompting, presenting examples of question-answer pairs, significantly enhances the performance of GPT-4
LLMs, particularly when combined with prompting enhancements and the correct legal texts, can perform at high levels of accuracy but not yet at expert tax lawyer levels
